summary: Jeff Atwood expresses frustration with a common annoyance caused by Microsoft's Windows Live Writer blogging tool. The software creates 'hidden' temporary posts for CSS style detection that frequently fail to delete themselves, polluting RSS feeds and search results. He notes over 19,500 Google results for these junk posts, calling out the poor implementation. The post is a brief, humorous complaint about software that inadvertently makes the internet worse rather than better.
key_points: Windows Live Writer creates temporary skeleton posts for style detection that are supposed to be hidden but frequently leak into public feeds
These junk posts pollute both RSS aggregators and Google search results, with over 19,500 indexed results
The ironic title suggests software meant to improve blogging is actually degrading internet quality
The issue stems from posts that should auto-delete but don't, requiring manual cleanup by users
money_quotes: Seriously. Enough with this already. I'm gonna hurt somebody.
Gee, thanks, Windows Live Writer. That's exactly 19,499 too many.
On the plus side, I did get the chance to bring two new GUIDs into the world.
key_insight: Software features that fail silently can pollute shared spaces at scale, turning a minor implementation oversight into thousands of pieces of internet garbage.
